title: Asclepias simplex
intro: Asclepias simplex, also known as Green Antelopehorn Milkweed, is a perennial plant species native to North America, belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including respiratory issues and skin irritations, due to its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The bioactive constituents of Asclepias simplex include cardenolides, glycosides, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include teas, tinctures, and infusions, often used to treat coughs, sore throats, and wounds.
taxonomy: Asclepias simplex is a member of the Asclepiadaceae family, which is now often classified under the family Apocynaceae due to the ongoing reclassification of plant families. This change reflects the evolving understanding of plant taxonomy. Asclepias simplex is a genus of flowering plants native to North America. The species is further classified as Asclepias simplex within the Asclepias genus. This classification is based on the plant's morphology, anatomy, and genetic characteristics.

side_effects: Asclepias simplex is native to North America and has been used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties. However, Asclepias simplex can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals when handled or ingested. The plant's milky sap can also cause eye irritation and other skin problems. Ingesting Asclepias simplex can lead to gastrointestinal issues and other systemic problems. As with any plant, it is essential to handle and use Asclepias simplex with caution and under the guidance of a medical professional.
